learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate personal engagement with texts by independently reading, critically analysing, and evaluating a range of contemporary literary texts in English, including works by First Nations authors
Recognise the social and cultural contexts of contemporary literature
Understand and deploy relevant critical concepts and demonstrate independent critical thinking
Demonstrate independent ability to communicate effectively verbally and in writing
Engage responsibly and ethically with the research process; demonstrate personal and engagement and independent research through appropriate methods and techniques
Work creatively and effectively with colleagues in the discipline

assessments.due_date:
Week 1 - Week 13

You will complete weekly activities in class as part of the internal Management of Learning assessment.
assessments.task_description:
The Management of Learning assessment involves weekly in-class activities run by your tutor. These tasks reward active participation and regular engagement. We strongly recommend you attend all tutorials, because this is where you will learn core skills in literary studies, engage in rich conversations about books and ideas, and develop peer review skills. 

Rationale: Consistent preparation and participation lead to better learning outcomes and more productive and enjoyable learning experiences. Through participation, humanities students gain highly valued and distinctive skills in critical thinking, discipline mastery, evidence-based learning, creativity, and collaboration. These skills are required by many workplaces.

Preparation: Because of our focus on reading and analysis, we expect you will come to tutorials well-prepared i.e. having read the set primary and secondary texts, attended or listened to the lectures, and developed some ideas and questions. You can also use the weekly Reading Guides (in each week's Learning Resources) to prepare for class. This will ensure you are individually prepared and ready to contribute to class discussion, and will ensure your tutorials are valuable learning experiences. 

How to succeed in this assessment item: Complete all readings before tutorials, be engaged in discussions, complete the tasks at hand and discuss them with peers, and ask questions.

Assessment: By participating in tutorials and actively engaging with the week's work in the set activities below, you can earn up to 25% towards your final grade. This provides your tutor with a chance to identify any problems you may be having in the course, and provide basic, regular feedback.

NB: All work for Management of Learning must be completed without the use of generative AI or machine translation software.

Structure: This assessment item is divided into three parts:

  1. Reading Logs (5%)
  2. Knowledge Quizzes (12%)
  3. Assessment Planning and Workshops (8%)


A detailed description of each part is available below:


Reading Logs (5%):

In weeks 1, 2, 4, 5, 12 participating in a tutorial by completing the week's reading log will earn you 1 mark each toward this assessment piece.

In tutorials, you will complete a 10-minute reading log task based on the week's Reading Guide questions. Your reading log entry should be 100-150 words (not including the question you are responding to) and take the form of a critical reflection on your reading of the week's texts. It needs no argument or formal structure, but must include at least one quotation from the set primary text. We will demonstrate how to submit these in our early lectures and tutorials. For practical tips on how to write reading log entries (also known as reflective journal entries) and other writing formats, see Student Services learning types of assignments. This learning activity ensures you are on track for your exam, and most students find this process an invaluable part of their learning.

How are my reading log entries marked? Reading logs are marked Pass / Fail on the Marking Criteria by your tutor. A rubric will be made available in blackboard.

How will I receive feedback? You will be provided with individual feedback through blackboard on your reading log entry during the first weeks of the semester. Tutors will provide group feedback during tutorials, as your responses will show us what the key issues are for each class.


Knowledge Quizzes (12%):

The knowledge quizzes are your management of learning task in weeks 6, 7, 8, and 9. Students must participate in their tutorial and answer 3 multiple choice questions each worth 1 point, provided in the tutorials (4 quizzes, each with three questions and thus three points available = total of 12 marks available). This work must be done by hand and without the aid of any modern technology. No extensions are possible. Make-up sessions will be run for students who are unable to attend for policy-approved reasons. This work ensures you are prepared for your research essay and are maintaining an appropriate pace through the course.

How are my knowledge quizzes marked? Knowledge quizzes are marked out of 3. You will earn one point for each multiple choice question answered correctly.

How will I receive feedback? Feedback will be provided in tutorials.


Assessment Planning and Workshops (8%):

Weeks 11 & 13

In week 11, you will participate in an editing and assessment check-in workshop in tutorials. This work is based on your upcoming research essay. To complete it, you must bring to class your thesis statement and a short sample paragraph for editing. This work is worth 6 points -- 3 for providing your own work, and 3 for critiquing someone else's. This work ensures you are prepared for your research essay and creative piece and are maintaining an appropriate pace through the course.

In week 13 you will submit a practise creative piece that responds to your week 12 reading log by rewriting a section of "Foster" indicated in tutorials. You must write on the correct section of the text. This work is worth 2 points, and will focus on editing and voice.

How is my assessment planning marked? This work is marked on a pass/fail basis. 3 points (pass/fail) are available for completing the critique workshop in class. 1 point each is available for providing your 1) thesis statement, 2) sample topic sentence, and 3) body evidence. Your creative practise will be marked pass/fail -- 1 point for completing the creative and 1 for the exegesis.

How will I receive feedback? Feedback will be provided via blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Complete your Reading Log and Assessment preparation exercised in class through Blackboard under the relevant weekly folder.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description: This formative assessment examines students' close reading and argumentative skills with reference to provided excerpts from Alison Whittaker's Blakwork (studied weeks 1&2) and passages from selected short stories studied in weeks 4&5. You will answer one short answer question and write a short essay using textual evidence from the two primary sources that will be provided in the examination paper. The essay must be correctly formatted with a bibliography in MLA 9th edition, guidelines for which are accessible through the UQ Library. Care should be given to expression, punctuation, and grammar. The exam paper must be undertaken without the use of generative AI or machine translation.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
The research essay requires students to write a comparative essay that analyses and engages with scholarly research on two texts studied in weeks 7-9. You will choose either Percival Everett's James AND/OR Kazuo Ishiguro's Klara and the Sun AND/OR Maria Tumarkin's Axiomatic to write a comparative essay. The task will involve close reading and analysis of scholarly research (criticism, commentary, theory), as well as of the literary texts. You must upload evidence of your personal engagement with the research sources you have consulted in the form of annotations on 1 page each from 3 secondary sources. Essay topics will be made available in Blackboard during semester. You will respond to one of these topics. The essay must be correctly formatted with a bibliography in MLA 9th edition, guidelines for which are accessible through the UQ Library. Care should be given to expression, punctuation, and grammar.

Use of Generative AI: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit via TurnItIn in the relevant Blackboard Assessment folder.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This is a creative writing task based on Claire Keegan's "Foster.". You will write a 500-word creative response, plus a 200-word exegesis explaining your creative process. Your response will be marked holistically for its overall effectiveness and grades will be given in response to the criteria (a detailed marking rubric will be provided through TurnItIn).

Use of Generative AI: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit via TurnItIn in the relevant Blackboard Assessment folder.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Lecture

Twenty-first century writing and reading.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: Lecture Twenty-first century writing and reading.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Tutorials begin

Welcome and an introduction to close reading, using Alison Whittaker's Blakwork . Please begin reading Blakwork and read Bennett and Royle’s “Studying Literature” in This Thing Called Literature (pp. 1-19 only) and O’Gorman and Eaglestone’s “Introduction” in The Routledge Companion to Twenty-First Century Literary Fiction (pp. 1–10).

Meet your tutor and your class: these colleagues will be your supportive learning community for the semester, and perhaps longer! Please bring your copy of Alison Whittaker's Blakwork, the Week 1 Reading Guide, and your own reading notes. These will become your discussion prompts. Weekly activities may include in-class close reading, discussion using the Socratic method, a writing log and/or quiz exercise based on the week's set text and secondary readings and assessment preparation. You need to have done the reading and attended (or listened to) the lecture before your tutorial. (This general advice applies to all tutorials.)

In tutorials this week, we will undertake our essay preparation activities, worth a total of 6%. Please bring to class 2 copies of your thesis statement and a sample paragraph for editing and workshopping. You will earn up to 3 points for your material provided and 3 points for participating in the workshop.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Tutorial

Creative writing + exegesis workshops

Please make sure you have read Claire Keegan's “Foster”. Continue reading the chapter "A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'" (p. 18-54) in George Saunders' A Swim in a Pond in the Rain (available through the library reading list).

In tutorials you will submit a 100-150 word creative piece that practises the skills you need to the final assessment. This work will focus on voice. You will also submit a 50 word exegesis on how this work speaks to the analytic work you did in your week 12 reading log. Each section is worth 1 point.

You will also submit a 50 word exegesis on how this work speaks to the analytic work you did in your week 12 reading log. Each section is worth 1 point.
